questions.title: Computer Network || 2015 NAT || 2-mark || Module 1: ISO/OSI Stack and SWP
questions.question: Suppose that the stop-and-wait protocol is used on a link with a bit rate of 64 kilobits per second and 20 milliseconds propagation delay. Assume that the transmission time for the acknowledgment and the processing time at nodes are negligible. Then the minimum frame size in bytes to achieve a link utilization of at least 50% is: [GATE||2015|| Set 1 ||NAT||2MARK]
questions.question_html: Suppose that the stop-and-wait protocol is used on a link with a bit rate of 64 kilobits per second and 20 milliseconds propagation delay. Assume that the transmission time for the acknowledgment and the processing time at nodes are negligible. Then the minimum frame size in bytes to achieve a link utilization of at least 50% is: [GATE||2015|| Set 1 ||NAT||2MARK]
questions.explanation:
efficiency = t _d / (t _d + 2×p _d )
For 50% efficiency:
1/2 = t _d / (t _d + 2×p _d )
t _d = (1/2) * (t _d + 2×p _d )
t _d = (1/2) * (t _d ) _ + p _d
t _d / 2 = p _d = 20 ms
t _d = 40 ms
Minimum frame size / 64Kbps = 40 ms
Minimum frame size = 64Kbps * 40 ms =2560 bits = 320 bytes
questions.explanation_html:
efficiency = td / (td + 2×pd )

For 50% efficiency:

1/2 = td / (td + 2×pd )

td = (1/2) * (td + 2×pd )

td = (1/2) * (td ) + pd

td / 2 = pd = 20 ms

td = 40 ms

Minimum frame size / 64Kbps = 40 ms

Minimum frame size = 64Kbps * 40 ms =2560 bits = 320 bytes

questions.question: Two hosts are connected via a packet switch with 10⁷ bits per second links. Each link has a propagation delay of 20 microseconds. The switch begins forwarding a packet 35 microseconds after it receives the same. If 10000 bits of data are to be transmitted between the two hosts using a packet size of 5000 bits, the time elapsed between the transmission of the first bit of data and the reception of the last bit of the data in microseconds is: [GATE||2015|| Set 3||NAT||2MARK]
questions.question_html: Two hosts are connected via a packet switch with 10⁷ bits per second links. Each link has a propagation delay of 20 microseconds. The switch begins forwarding a packet 35 microseconds after it receives the same. If 10000 bits of data are to be transmitted between the two hosts using a packet size of 5000 bits, the time elapsed between the transmission of the first bit of data and the reception of the last bit of the data in microseconds is: [GATE||2015|| Set 3||NAT||2MARK]
questions.explanation:
Extra delay at switch = 35 microseconds for each packet.
Data = 10000 bits
Number of packets = 10000/5000=2 packets
Transmission delay for one packet = 500 microseconds.
- At t=500 μsec, last bit of packet 1 is placed on link 1 by A and transmission of packet 1 begins.
- At t=520 μsec, last bit of packet 1 reaches switch.
- At t=555 μsec, first bit of packet 1 is placed on link 2 by the switch.
- At t=1000 μsec, last bit of packet 2 is placed on link 1 by A.
- At t=1020 μsec, last bit of packet 2 reaches switch.
- Note: Packet 2 needs to wait until 1055 μsec before the switch transfers it.
- Last bit of packet 1 will be placed on link 2 by switch at 1055 μsec.
- Hence, no additional delay for packet 2.
- At t=1055 μsec, packet 2’s first bit is placed on link 2.
- At t=1575 μsec, last bit of packet 2 reaches B.
- ∴1575 μsec is required.
questions.explanation_html:
Extra delay at switch = 35 microseconds for each packet.

Data = 10000 bits

Number of packets = 10000/5000=2 packets

Transmission delay for one packet = 500 microseconds.

  • At t=500 μsec, last bit of packet 1 is placed on link 1 by A and transmission of packet 1 begins.
  • At t=520 μsec, last bit of packet 1 reaches switch.
  • At t=555 μsec, first bit of packet 1 is placed on link 2 by the switch.
  • At t=1000 μsec, last bit of packet 2 is placed on link 1 by A.
  • At t=1020 μsec, last bit of packet 2 reaches switch.
  • Note: Packet 2 needs to wait until 1055 μsec before the switch transfers it.
  • Last bit of packet 1 will be placed on link 2 by switch at 1055 μsec.
  • Hence, no additional delay for packet 2.
  • At t=1055 μsec, packet 2’s first bit is placed on link 2.
  • At t=1575 μsec, last bit of packet 2 reaches B.


  • ∴1575 μsec is required.

questions.title: Computer Network || 2021 NAT || 2-mark || Module 1: ISO/OSI Stack and SWP
questions.question:
Consider the sliding window flow-control protocol operating between a sender and a receiver over a full-duplex error-free link. Assume the following:
- The time taken for processing the data frame by the receiver is negligible.
- The time taken for processing the acknowledgement frame by the sender is negligible.
- The sender has an infinite number of frames available for transmission.
- The size of the data frame is 2000 bits, and the size of the acknowledgement frame is 10 bits.
- The link data rate in each direction is 1 Mbps (= 10 ^6 bits per second).
- One-way propagation delay of the link is 100 milliseconds.
The minimum value of the sender’s window size in terms of the number of frames, (rounded to the nearest integer) needed to achieve a link utilization of 50% is _______. [ GATE||2021||SET1|| NAT || 2-mark ]
questions.question_html:
Consider the sliding window flow-control protocol operating between a sender and a receiver over a full-duplex error-free link. Assume the following:

  • The time taken for processing the data frame by the receiver is negligible.
  • The time taken for processing the acknowledgement frame by the sender is negligible.
  • The sender has an infinite number of frames available for transmission.
  • The size of the data frame is 2000 bits, and the size of the acknowledgement frame is 10 bits.
  • The link data rate in each direction is 1 Mbps (= 106 bits per second).
  • One-way propagation delay of the link is 100 milliseconds.

The minimum value of the sender’s window size in terms of the number of frames, (rounded to the nearest integer) needed to achieve a link utilization of 50% is _______. [ GATE||2021||SET1|| NAT || 2-mark ]
questions.explanation:
The time taken for a packet ( Tt(packet) ):
Tt(packet)=L/B.W=2000 bits/10 ^6  bps=2×10 ^−3  sec=2 msec
The time taken for an acknowledgement ( Tt(Ack) ):
Tt(Ack) = 10 bits/10 ^6  bps = 10 ^−5  sec=10 ^−2  msec=0.01
Tp=100 msec
The total time ( Total time ):
Totaltime=Tt(packet)+Tp+Tt(Ack)=2 msec+100 msec+0.01 msec=102.01
Efficiency Calculation:
Efficiency=1/2=Useful time/Total time
2×n=102.012
n = 102.01/2 = 51.005
For the minimum value, we round up to the nearest integer, hence the window size is 51 .
questions.explanation_html:
The time taken for a packet (Tt(packet)):

Tt(packet)=L/B.W=2000 bits/106 bps=2×10−3 sec=2 msec

The time taken for an acknowledgement (Tt(Ack)):

Tt(Ack) = 10 bits/106 bps = 10−5 sec=10−2 msec=0.01 

Tp=100 msec

The total time (Total time):

Totaltime=Tt(packet)+Tp+Tt(Ack)=2 msec+100 msec+0.01 msec=102.01 

Efficiency Calculation:

Efficiency=1/2=Useful time/Total time

2×n=102.012

n = 102.01/2 = 51.005

For the minimum value, we round up to the nearest integer, hence the window size is 51.

Assume that a 12-bit Hamming codeword consisting of 8-bit data and 4 check bits is transmitted. The data bits are denoted by d8,d7,d6,d5,d4,d3,d2,d1, and the check bits are given in the following tables:
Data Bits d8 d7 d6 d5 d4 d3 d2 d1 1 1 0 x 0 1 0 1 Check Bits c8 c4 c2 c1 y 0 1 0 Which one of the following choices gives the correct values of x and y? [GATE||2021 ||SET1||MCQ || 2-mark ]

questions.explanation:
Hamming code is 12 bit : d12 d11 d10 d9 c8 d7 d6 d5 c4 d3 c2 c1
d12 d11 d10 d9 d7 d6 d5 d3 1 1 0 x 0 1 0 1
c8 c4 c2 c1 y 0 1 0
convert all data bits to binary form in parity bits
d12 = c8 + c4
d11 = c8 + c2 + c1
d10 = c8 + c2
d9 = c8 + c1
d7 = c4 + c2+ c1
d6 = c4+c2
d5 = c4+c1
d3 = c2+c1
//collect data bits where ci is coming
c8 = d12, d11,d10,d9
c4 = d12,d7,d6,d5
c2 = d11,d10,d7,d6,d3
c1 = d11,d9,d7,d5,d3
all check bits/parity bits should be even parity
c1 = d11+d9+d7+d5+d3  ---> even parity
c1 = 1+x+0+0+1
c1 = 2+x ---> for even parity x =0;
c8 = d12+d11+d10+d9 ---> c8 should be even parity
c8 =y
c8 = 1+1+0+x
c8 = 2+x ---> c8 = 2+0
for c8 to be even parity c8 =0 i.e y=0
So ans is x=0 and y=0

questions.title: Computer Network || 2012 MCQ || 2-mark || Module 2: LAN
questions.question: Consider a source computer (S) transmitting a file of size 10 ^6 bits to a destination computer (D) over a network of two routers (R1 and R2) and three links (L1, L2, and L3). L1 connects S to R1; L2 connects R1 to R2; and L3 connects R2 to D. Let each link be of length 100 km. Assume signals travel over each link at a speed of 10 ^8 meters per second. Assume that the link bandwidth on each link is 1 Mbps. Let the file be broken down into 1000 packets, each of size 1000 bits. Find the total sum of transmission and propagation delays in transmitting the file from S to D? [GATE|| 2012 MCQ || 2-mark ]
questions.question_html: Consider a source computer (S) transmitting a file of size 106 bits to a destination computer (D) over a network of two routers (R1 and R2) and three links (L1, L2, and L3). L1 connects S to R1; L2 connects R1 to R2; and L3 connects R2 to D. Let each link be of length 100 km. Assume signals travel over each link at a speed of 108meters per second. Assume that the link bandwidth on each link is 1 Mbps. Let the file be broken down into 1000 packets, each of size 1000 bits. Find the total sum of transmission and propagation delays in transmitting the file from S to D? [GATE|| 2012 MCQ || 2-mark ]
questions.explanation:
Routers are store and forward devices.
- Propagation time = 100 km/10 ^8  m/s = 1 ms
- Transmission time for a packet = 1000 bits/10 ^6  bits/sec = 1 ms
Packets will be forwarded in a pipelined manner after the first packet reaches the receiver, with every 1 ms a new one arriving.
Now, the time taken by packet number 1 to reach the destination is:
1 ms (Tx at sender) + 1 ms (Tp from sender to R1) + 1 ms (Tx at R1)
- 1 ms (Tp from R1 to R2) + 1 ms (Tx at R2) + 1 ms (Tp from R2 to destination) = 6 ms
So, for packet 1000:
6 ms + 999 ms = 1005 ms
questions.explanation_html:
Routers are store and forward devices.

  • Propagation time = 100 km/108 m/s = 1 ms
  • Transmission time for a packet = 1000 bits/106 bits/sec = 1 ms

Packets will be forwarded in a pipelined manner after the first packet reaches the receiver, with every 1 ms a new one arriving.

Now, the time taken by packet number 1 to reach the destination is:

1 ms (Tx at sender) + 1 ms (Tp from sender to R1) + 1 ms (Tx at R1)

  • 1 ms (Tp from R1 to R2) + 1 ms (Tx at R2) + 1 ms (Tp from R2 to destination) = 6 ms

So, for packet 1000:
6 ms + 999 ms = 1005 ms

questions.question: Consider a LAN with four nodes S1,S2,S3,S4​. Time is divided into fixed-size slots, and a node can begin its transmission only at the beginning of a slot. A collision is said to have occurred if more than one node transmits in the same slot. The probabilities of generation of a frame in a time slot by S1,S2,S3 and S4 are 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, and 0.4, respectively. The probability of sending a frame in the first slot without any collision by any of these four stations is __________. [GATE||2015||Set 1|||NAT||2MARK]
questions.question_html: Consider a LAN with four nodes S1,S2,S3,S4​. Time is divided into fixed-size slots, and a node can begin its transmission only at the beginning of a slot. A collision is said to have occurred if more than one node transmits in the same slot. The probabilities of generation of a frame in a time slot by S1,S2,S3 and S4 are 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, and 0.4, respectively. The probability of sending a frame in the first slot without any collision by any of these four stations is __________. [GATE||2015||Set 1|||NAT||2MARK]
questions.explanation:
The probability of sending a frame in the first slot without any collision by any of these four stations is sum of following 4 probabilities
Probability that S1 sends a frame and no one else does + Probability thatS2 sends a frame and no one else does + Probability thatS3 sends a frame and no one else does +
Probability thatS4 sends a frame and no one else does
= 0.1 * (1 - 0.2) * (1 - 0.3) *(1 - 0.4) + (1 -0.1) * 0.2 * (1 - 0.3) *(1 - 0.4) + (1 -0.1) * (1 - 0.2) * 0.3 *(1 - 0.4) + (1 -0.1) * (1 - 0.2) * (1 - 0.3) * 0.4
= 0.4404

questions.title: Computer Network || 2018 NAT || 2-mark || Module 2: LAN
questions.question:
Consider a simple communication system where multiple nodes are connected by a shared broadcast medium (like Ethernet or wireless).
The nodes in the system use the following carrier-sense based medium access protocol.
A node that receives a packet to transmit will carrier-sense the medium for 5 units of time.
If the node does not detect any other transmission in this duration, it starts transmitting its packet in the next time unit.
If the node detects another transmission, it waits until this other transmission finishes, and then begins to carrier-sense for 5 time units again.
Once they start to transmit, nodes do not perform any collision detection and continue transmission even if a collision occurs.
All transmissions last for 20 units of time.
Assume that the transmission signal travels at the speed of 10 meters per unit time in the medium.
Assume that the system has two nodes P and Q, located at a distance d meters from each other.
P starts transmitting a packet at time t=0 after successfully completing its carrier-sense phase. Node Q has a packet to transmit at time t=0 and begins to carrier-sense the medium.
The maximum distance d (in meters, rounded to the closest integer) that allows Q to successfully avoid a collision between its proposed transmission and P's ongoing transmission is _______. [GATE|| 2018 NAT || 2-mark]
questions.question_html:
Consider a simple communication system where multiple nodes are connected by a shared broadcast medium (like Ethernet or wireless).

The nodes in the system use the following carrier-sense based medium access protocol.

A node that receives a packet to transmit will carrier-sense the medium for 5 units of time.

If the node does not detect any other transmission in this duration, it starts transmitting its packet in the next time unit.

If the node detects another transmission, it waits until this other transmission finishes, and then begins to carrier-sense for 5 time units again.

Once they start to transmit, nodes do not perform any collision detection and continue transmission even if a collision occurs.

All transmissions last for 20 units of time.

Assume that the transmission signal travels at the speed of 10 meters per unit time in the medium.

Assume that the system has two nodes P and Q, located at a distance d meters from each other.

P starts transmitting a packet at time t=0 after successfully completing its carrier-sense phase. Node Q has a packet to transmit at time t=0 and begins to carrier-sense the medium.

The maximum distance d (in meters, rounded to the closest integer) that allows Q to successfully avoid a collision between its proposed transmission and P's ongoing transmission is _______. [GATE|| 2018 NAT || 2-mark]
questions.explanation:
At time t=0, station P starts transmitting packet (since it already senses the channel is free). At the same time, station Q is sensing the channel to check if the channel is free or not.
To avoid collision:
Sensing of node Q ≥ Transmitting a packet
i.e., if the channel is found free at 5 unit time or more than 5 unit time, station Q data will be collied with station P anywhere in the middle.
So, the distance between P and Q = sensing of node Q ≥ transmitting a packet.
d=5 time unit
d=5 unit × 10 meter/unit
d=50 meter
Note: The idea is simple, the 1st bit of node P data reaches node Q within 5 time units or at exact 5 time unit, then node Q understands that someone is transmitting the data and waits for the next time unit to sense the channel is free or not.
questions.explanation_html:
At time t=0, station P starts transmitting packet (since it already senses the channel is free). At the same time, station Q is sensing the channel to check if the channel is free or not.

To avoid collision:

Sensing of node Q ≥ Transmitting a packet
i.e., if the channel is found free at 5 unit time or more than 5 unit time, station Q data will be collied with station P anywhere in the middle.

So, the distance between P and Q = sensing of node Q ≥ transmitting a packet.

d=5 time unit

d=5 unit × 10 meter/unit

d=50 meter

Note: The idea is simple, the 1st bit of node P data reaches node Q within 5 time units or at exact 5 time unit, then node Q understands that someone is transmitting the data and waits for the next time unit to sense the channel is free or not.

questions.title: Computer Network || 2019 MCQ || 2-mark || Module 2: LAN
questions.question: Suppose that in an IP-over-Ethernet network, a machine X wishes to find the MAC address of another machine Y in its subnet. Which one of the following techniques can be used for this? [GATE|| 2019 MCQ || 2-mark]
questions.question_html: Suppose that in an IP-over-Ethernet network, a machine X wishes to find the MAC address of another machine Y in its subnet. Which one of the following techniques can be used for this? [GATE|| 2019 MCQ || 2-mark]

options.text: X sends an ARP request packet with broadcast IP address in its local subnet
options.html: X sends an ARP request packet with broadcast IP address in its local subnet

options.text: X sends an ARP request packet to the local gateway’s MAC address which then finds the MAC address of Y and sends to X
options.html: X sends an ARP request packet to the local gateway’s MAC address which then finds the MAC address of Y and sends to X

options.text: X sends an ARP request packet with broadcast MAC address in its local subnet
options.html: X sends an ARP request packet with broadcast MAC address in its local subnet

options.text: X sends an ARP request packet to the local gateway’s IP address which then finds the MAC address of Y and sends to X
options.html: X sends an ARP request packet to the local gateway’s IP address which then finds the MAC address of Y and sends to X

questions.explanation:
Steps in ARP Operation :
- The sender (X) knows its own IP address and  MAC address. X also knows the IP address of the target (Y) . It needs to find MAC address of Y
- IP asks ARP to create an ARP request message, filling in X's IP and MAC address and Y's IP address.  The   destination   MAC   address   is   set   to   all   0s .
- The message is passed to Data Link layer where it is encapsulated in a frame using MAC address of X as the source address and  physical   broadcast   address (all 1s)   as the destination address.
- Every host in subnet receives the request message because we have used broadcast MAC address in the destination address. All machines except Y (we have specified Y's IP address in our ARP request message) drop the ARP request message.
- Y replies with an ARP reply message that contains its MAC address.
- When X receives this message, it gets to know the MAC address of Y.
Please note that, a host uses its own IP address and network mask to decide if target IP address is in its own network or not.
If it is in its network, it uses ARP to resolve the MAC address.
If target IP address is not in its network, it takes the help of default gateway to resolve MAC address using ARP.
Since in question it is clearly mentioned that X (source) and Y (destination) both are in the same subnet, we need not send ARP request message targeted at gateway and then expect it to give the MAC address of Y to X.
questions.explanation_html:
Steps in ARP Operation : 

  1. The sender (X) knows its own IP address and  MAC address. X also knows the IP address of the target (Y) . It needs to find MAC address of Y
  2. IP asks ARP to create an ARP request message, filling in X's IP and MAC address and Y's IP address. The destination MAC address is set to all 0s.
  3. The message is passed to Data Link layer where it is encapsulated in a frame using MAC address of X as the source address and physical broadcast address (all 1s)  as the destination address.
  4. Every host in subnet receives the request message because we have used broadcast MAC address in the destination address. All machines except Y (we have specified Y's IP address in our ARP request message) drop the ARP request message. 
  5. Y replies with an ARP reply message that contains its MAC address.
  6. When X receives this message, it gets to know the MAC address of Y.

Please note that, a host uses its own IP address and network mask to decide if target IP address is in its own network or not.

If it is in its network, it uses ARP to resolve the MAC address.

If target IP address is not in its network, it takes the help of default gateway to resolve MAC address using ARP.

Since in question it is clearly mentioned that X (source) and Y (destination) both are in the same subnet, we need not send ARP request message targeted at gateway and then expect it to give the MAC address of Y to X.

questions.title: Computer Network || 2021 NAT || 2-mark || Module 2: LAN
questions.question: Consider a network using the pure ALOHA medium access control protocol, where each frame is of length 1,000 bits. The channel transmission rate is 1 Mbps (i.e., 10610^6106 bits per second). The aggregate number of transmissions across all the nodes (including new transmissions and retransmitted frames due to collisions) is modeled as a Poisson process with a rate of 1,000 frames per second. Throughput is defined as the average number of frames successfully transmitted per second. The throughput of the network (rounded to the nearest integer) is __________. [GATE||2021 Set 2 ||NAT||2MARKS]
questions.question_html: Consider a network using the pure ALOHA medium access control protocol, where each frame is of length 1,000 bits. The channel transmission rate is 1 Mbps (i.e., 10610^6106 bits per second). The aggregate number of transmissions across all the nodes (including new transmissions and retransmitted frames due to collisions) is modeled as a Poisson process with a rate of 1,000 frames per second. Throughput is defined as the average number of frames successfully transmitted per second. The throughput of the network (rounded to the nearest integer) is __________. [GATE||2021 Set 2 ||NAT||2MARKS]
questions.explanation:
Frame length = 1000 bits
Channel rate = 1 Mbps = 10⁶ bits/s
Frame transmission time T = 1000 / 10⁶ = 0.001 s
Aggregate attempt rate (new + retransmissions) = 1000 frames/s
Offered load (G):
G = λ × T = 1000 × 0.001 = 1
Pure ALOHA throughput formula:
S = G · e^(−2G)
Substitute G = 1:
S = 1 · e^(−2) ≈ 0.1353 frames per frame-time
Convert to frames per second:
Throughput = 0.1353 / 0.001 ≈ 135.3 frames/s
Final Answer (rounded to nearest integer):
135 frames per second

questions.title: Computer Network || 2012 MCQ || 2-mark || Module 3:TCP, UDP and IP
questions.question: An Internet Service Provider (ISP) has the following chunk of CIDR-based IP addresses available with it: 245.248.128.0/20. The ISP wants to give half of this chunk of addresses to Organization A, and a quarter to Organization B, while retaining the remaining with itself. Which of the following is a valid allocation of addresses to A and B? [GATE|| 2012 MCQ || 2-mark ]
questions.question_html: An Internet Service Provider (ISP) has the following chunk of CIDR-based IP addresses available with it: 245.248.128.0/20. The ISP wants to give half of this chunk of addresses to Organization A, and a quarter to Organization B, while retaining the remaining with itself. Which of the following is a valid allocation of addresses to A and B? [GATE|| 2012 MCQ || 2-mark ]
questions.explanation:
The prefix is given is 20. So, the first 20 bits denote the network id bits, and the next 12 bits indicate host bits. To divide the network into two halves, we borrow bits from the host bits. Hence, for half (2 partitions), the prefix will be 20 + 1 and for 4 partitions, the prefix will be 20 + 2 = 22. So prefix for A will be 21 and prefix for B will be 22. This eliminates options C and D.
For A, we need to consider the 21st bit. It can have values 0 or 1. Let's take its value as 1. So, the network id becomes 245.248.136.0/21.
For B, we need to consider the 21st and 22nd bits. We can have four possibilities: 00, 01, 10, 11. Out of these, 10, and 11 belong to network A. So, we take 00 ie 245.248. 128 .0/22 (Network B) and 01 as 245.248. 132 .0/22.

This eliminates options C and D.

For A, we need to consider the 21st bit. It can have values 0 or 1. Let's take its value as 1. So, the network id becomes 245.248.136.0/21.

For B, we need to consider the 21st and 22nd bits. We can have four possibilities: 00, 01, 10, 11. Out of these, 10, and 11 belong to network A. So, we take 00 ie 245.248.128.0/22 (Network B) and 01 as 245.248.132.0/22.

questions.question: Consider an instance of TCP's Additive Increase Multiplicative Decrease (AIMD) algorithm where the windows size at the start of the slow start phase is 2 MSS and the threshold at the start of the first transmission is 8 MSS. Assume that a time-out occurs during the fifth transmission. Find the congestion window size at the end of the tenth transmission. [GATE|| 2012 MCQ || 2-mark ]
questions.question_html: Consider an instance of TCP's Additive Increase Multiplicative Decrease (AIMD) algorithm where the windows size at the start of the slow start phase is 2 MSS and the threshold at the start of the first transmission is 8 MSS. Assume that a time-out occurs during the fifth transmission. Find the congestion window size at the end of the tenth transmission. [GATE|| 2012 MCQ || 2-mark ]
questions.explanation:
Since  Slow Start  is used, window size is increased by the number of segments successfully sent. This happens until either threshold value is reached or time out occurs.
In both of the above situations  AIMD  is used to avoid congestion. If threshold is reached, window size will be increased linearly. If there is timeout, window size will be reduced to half.
Window size for 1st transmission = 2 MSS
Window size for 2nd transmission = 4 MSS
Window size for 3rd transmission = 8 MSS
threshold reached, increase linearly (according to AIMD)
Window size for 4th transmission = 9 MSS
Window size for 5th transmission = 10 MSS
time out occurs, resend 5th with window size starts with as slow start.
Window size for 6th transmission = 1 MSS
Window size for 7th transmission = 2 MSS
Window size for 8th transmission = 4 MSS
threshold reached, now increase linearly (according to AIMD)
Additive Increase: 5 MSS (since 8 MSS isn’t permissible anymore)
Window size for 9th transmission = 5 MSS
Window size for 10th transmission = 6 MSS
Window size at the end of 10th transmission = 6+1 =7 MSS

questions.question:
Host A (on TCP/IP v4 network A) sends an IP datagram to host B (also on TCP/IP v4 network B). Assume that no error occurred during the transmission of D. When D reaches B, which of the following IP header field(s) may be different from that of the original datagram D?
(a) TTL
(b) Checksum
(c) Fragment Offset [GATE||2014|| Set 3||MCQ||1MARK ]
questions.question_html:
Host A (on TCP/IP v4 network A) sends an IP datagram to host B (also on TCP/IP v4 network B). Assume that no error occurred during the transmission of D. When D reaches B, which of the following IP header field(s) may be different from that of the original datagram D?

(a) TTL
(b) Checksum
(c) Fragment Offset [GATE||2014|| Set 3||MCQ||1MARK ]
questions.explanation:
Whenever an IP packet is transmitted, the value in Time to Live (TTL) field will be decremented on every single hop. Hence, TTL is changed on every hop.
Now, since TTL changes, hence the Checksum of the packet will also change.
For the Fragmentation offset, A packet will be fragmented if the packet has a size greater than the Maximum Transmission Unit (MTU) of the network. Hence, Fragmentation offset can also be changed.
The Correct answer is OPTION D.

A user on host A sends a file of size 10^3 bytes to host B through routers R1 and R2 in three different ways. In the first case, a single packet containing the complete file is transmitted from A to B. In the second case, the file is transmitted from A to B. In the third case, the file is split into 20 equal parts and these packets are sent from A to B. Each packet contains 100 bytes of header information along with the user data. Consider only transmission time and ignore processing, queuing and propagation delays. Also assume that there are no errors during transmission. Let T1, T2, and T3 be the times taken to transmit the file in the first, second, and third case respectively. Which one of the following is CORRECT? [GATE||2014|| Set 2 ||MCQ||2MARK]
.

T₃ = (3 × 1.2) + (19 × 1.2) = 26.4 msec
T₁ = T₃ = 26.4 msec
T _2 < T _1 =T₃

questions.title: Computer Network || 2015 MCQ || 1-mark || Module 3:TCP, UDP and IP
questions.question:
Suppose two hosts use a TCP connection to transfer a large file. Which of the following statements is/are FALSE with respect to the TCP connection?
- If the sequence number of a segment is m, then the sequence number of the subsequent segment is always m+1.
- If the estimated round trip time at any given point of time is t sec, the value of the retransmission timeout is always set to greater than or equal to t sec.
- The size of the advertised window never changes during the course of the TCP connection.
- The number of unacknowledged bytes at the sender is always less than or equal to the advertised window. [ GATE||2015||Set 1 ||MCQ||1 MARKS]
questions.question_html:
Suppose two hosts use a TCP connection to transfer a large file. Which of the following statements is/are FALSE with respect to the TCP connection?

  1. If the sequence number of a segment is m, then the sequence number of the subsequent segment is always m+1.
  2. If the estimated round trip time at any given point of time is t sec, the value of the retransmission timeout is always set to greater than or equal to t sec.
  3. The size of the advertised window never changes during the course of the TCP connection.
  4. The number of unacknowledged bytes at the sender is always less than or equal to the advertised window. [ GATE||2015||Set 1 ||MCQ||1 MARKS]
questions.explanation:
Statement 1 is false because, the sequence number of the subsequent segment depends on the number of byte characters in the current segment.
Statement 2 is true, because RTO timer is always ≥ Estimated RTT.
Statement 3 is false. Receiver window is used to give the sender an idea of how much free buffer space is available at the receiver.
Statement 4 is true, TCP is not permitted to overflow the allocated receiver buffer.
Hence, when the sender cannot send any more data, receiver window would be 0 and hence, the buffer would have unacknowledged data.
questions.explanation_html:
Statement 1 is false because, the sequence number of the subsequent segment depends on the number of byte characters in the current segment.
Statement 2 is true, because RTO timer is always ≥ Estimated RTT.
Statement 3 is false. Receiver window is used to give the sender an idea of how much free buffer space is available at the receiver.
Statement 4 is true, TCP is not permitted to overflow the allocated receiver buffer.
Hence, when the sender cannot send any more data, receiver window would be 0 and hence, the buffer would have unacknowledged data.

questions.title: Computer Network || 2017 NAT || 1-mark || Module 3:TCP, UDP and IP
questions.question: The maximum number of IPv4 router addresses that can be listed in the record route (RR) option field of an IPv4 header is __________. [ GATE||2017||Set 2|| NAT || 1-mark ]
questions.question_html: The maximum number of IPv4 router addresses that can be listed in the record route (RR) option field of an IPv4 header is __________. [ GATE||2017||Set 2|| NAT || 1-mark ]
questions.explanation:
Record route is an optional field used to record address.
In IPv4 header, 40 bytes are reserved for OPTIONS.
For Record Route to stores, 1 byte is used to store type of option, 1 byte for length and 1 byte for pointer. Out of 40, 37 bytes are left.
An IP4 address takes 32 bits or 4 bytes.
We can store at most floor(37/4) = 9 router addresses.
questions.explanation_html:
Record route is an optional field used to record address. 
In IPv4 header, 40 bytes are reserved for OPTIONS.

For Record Route to stores, 1 byte is used to store type of option, 1 byte for length and 1 byte for pointer. Out of 40, 37 bytes are left. 
An IP4 address takes 32 bits or 4 bytes. 
We can store at most floor(37/4) = 9 router addresses.

questions.title: Computer Network || 2017 MCQ || 1-mark || Module 3:TCP, UDP and IP
questions.question: Consider a TCP client and a TCP server running on two different machines. After completing data transfer, the TCP client calls close to terminate the connection and a FIN segment is sent to the TCP server. Server-side TCP responds by sending an ACK, which is received by the client-side TCP. As per the TCP connection state diagram (RFC 793), in which state does the client-side TCP connection wait for the FIN from the server-side TCP? [GATE||2017|| Set 1 || MCQ || 1-mark ]
questions.question_html: Consider a TCP client and a TCP server running on two different machines. After completing data transfer, the TCP client calls close to terminate the connection and a FIN segment is sent to the TCP server. Server-side TCP responds by sending an ACK, which is received by the client-side TCP. As per the TCP connection state diagram (RFC 793), in which state does the client-side TCP connection wait for the FIN from the server-side TCP? [GATE||2017|| Set 1 || MCQ || 1-mark ]
questions.explanation:
FIN WAIT-1 -->  if client sent FIN segment to the server and waiting for the ACK of its own FIN segment
Now after FIN WAIT-1 there are two possibility --
(1) if client receives ACK for its own FIN then client move to FIN-wait-2 and waiting for FIN from server side
after reciving the FIN from server client will send ACK   then connection is not closed immediately it wait for some time and then closed  and this wait time is called TIME-WAIT state.
(2) if client not recive ACK instead of ACK if it recived FIN from server side now client will ackonwledge this FIN and move to close state
Here Client will wait for the ACK for its own FIN.
After receiving ACK, client will move to TIME-WAIT state.
questions.explanation_html:
FIN WAIT-1 -->  if client sent FIN segment to the server and waiting for the ACK of its own FIN segment

Now after FIN WAIT-1 there are two possibility -- 

(1) if client receives ACK for its own FIN then client move to FIN-wait-2 and waiting for FIN from server side

after reciving the FIN from server client will send ACK   then connection is not closed immediately it wait for some time and then closed  and this wait time is called TIME-WAIT state.

(2) if client not recive ACK instead of ACK if it recived FIN from server side now client will ackonwledge this FIN and move to close state

Here Client will wait for the ACK for its own FIN.
After receiving ACK, client will move to TIME-WAIT state.

questions.question: Consider a long-lived TCP session with an end-to-end bandwidth of 1 Gbps = (10^9 bits per second). The session starts with a sequence number of 1234. The minimum time (in seconds, rounded to the closest integer) before this sequence number can be used again is [GATE||2018 NAT || 1-mark]
questions.question_html: Consider a long-lived TCP session with an end-to-end bandwidth of 1 Gbps = (10^9 bits per second). The session starts with a sequence number of 1234. The minimum time (in seconds, rounded to the closest integer) before this sequence number can be used again is [GATE||2018 NAT || 1-mark]
questions.explanation:
remember tcp is byte(not bit) stream protocol
sequence number field of tcp header is 32 bit, so only (2 ^32 =4G) sequence number available so wrap time is after how much time we use same sequence number
bandwidth = 1Gbps = 10 ^9 / 8 Byte per second
10^9/8 sequence no. consume - 1 sec ( since bandwidth=10 ^9 /8)
1 seq no. consume - 8 / 10 ^9 sec
2 ^32 seq no. consume - 2 ^ 32 * (8 / 10 ^9 )  sec = 34.35  sec (approx 34 sec)
questions.explanation_html:
remember tcp is byte(not bit) stream protocol

sequence number field of tcp header is 32 bit, so only (232=4G) sequence number available so wrap time is after how much time we use same sequence number

bandwidth = 1Gbps = 109 / 8 Byte per second

10^9/8 sequence no. consume - 1 sec ( since bandwidth=109/8)

1 seq no. consume - 8 / 109 sec

232 seq no. consume - 2 ^ 32 * (8 / 109)  sec = 34.35  sec (approx 34 sec)

questions.question: Consider three machines M, N, and P with IP addresses 100.10.5.2, 100.10.5.5, and 100.10.5.6 respectively. The subnet mask is set to 255.255.255.252 for all the three machines. Which one of the following is true? [GATE|| 2019 MCQ || 2-mark]
questions.question_html: Consider three machines M, N, and P with IP addresses 100.10.5.2, 100.10.5.5, and 100.10.5.6 respectively. The subnet mask is set to 255.255.255.252 for all the three machines. Which one of the following is true? [GATE|| 2019 MCQ || 2-mark]

options.text: M, N and P all belong to the same subnet
options.html: M, N and P all belong to the same subnet

options.text: Only N and P belong to the same subnet
options.html: Only N and P belong to the same subnet

options.text: M, N, and P belong to three different subnets
options.html: M, N, and P belong to three different subnets

options.text: Only M and N belong to the same subnet
options.html: Only M and N belong to the same subnet

questions.explanation:
We can avoid BITWISE ANDing here. The concept is, in subnet mask we need to take a note of positions of set bits. For those positions,  in IP addresses we should've matching bits.
here, 1st, 2nd and 3rd octets of M,N,P are same. Their 4th octet is given as
M: 0000 0010; N: 0000 0101; P: 0000 0110
4th octet of Subnet Mask: 1111 1100
It means, we should've matching bits for starting 6 bits in given IP addresses.
Only N and P satisfies this. B is the correct option here.

questions.title: Computer Network || 2023 MSQ || 2-mark || Module 3:TCP, UDP and IP
questions.question:
Suppose in a web browser, you click on the www . gate-2023 . in URL. The browser cache is empty. The IP address for this URL is not cached in your local host, so a DNS lookup is triggered (by the local DNS server deployed on your local host) over the 3-tier DNS hierarchy in an iterative mode. No resource records are cached anywhere across all DNS servers.
Let RTT denote the round trip time between your local host and DNS servers in the DNS hierarchy. The round trip time between the local host and the web server hosting www.gate-2023.in is also equal to RTT. The HTML file associated with the URL is small enough to have negligible transmission time and negligible rendering time by your web browser, which references 10 equally small objects on the same web server.
Which of the following statements is/are CORRECT about the minimum elapsed time between clicking on the URL and your browser fully rendering it? [GATE || 2023 MSQ || 2-mark]
questions.question_html:
Suppose in a web browser, you click on the www . gate-2023 . in URL. The browser cache is empty. The IP address for this URL is not cached in your local host, so a DNS lookup is triggered (by the local DNS server deployed on your local host) over the 3-tier DNS hierarchy in an iterative mode. No resource records are cached anywhere across all DNS servers.
Let RTT denote the round trip time between your local host and DNS servers in the DNS hierarchy. The round trip time between the local host and the web server hosting www.gate-2023.in is also equal to RTT. The HTML file associated with the URL is small enough to have negligible transmission time and negligible rendering time by your web browser, which references 10 equally small objects on the same web server.
Which of the following statements is/are CORRECT about the minimum elapsed time between clicking on the URL and your browser fully rendering it? [GATE || 2023 MSQ || 2-mark]

options.text: 7 RTTs, in case of non-persistent HTTP with 5 parallel TCP connections.
options.html: 7 RTTs, in case of non-persistent HTTP with 5 parallel TCP connections.

options.text: 5 RTTs, in case of persistent HTTP with pipelining.
options.html: 5 RTTs, in case of persistent HTTP with pipelining.

options.text: 9 RTTs, in case of non-persistent HTTP with 5 parallel TCP connections.
options.html: 9 RTTs, in case of non-persistent HTTP with 5 parallel TCP connections.

options.text: 6 RTTs, in case of persistent HTTP with pipelining.
options.html: 6 RTTs, in case of persistent HTTP with pipelining.

questions.explanation:
persistent http with pipelining will take 3 RTTs (one for connection + one for opening web page + one for 10 objects)
And in iterative mode for DNS is 3 RTTs (Root + Top domain + Authoritative).
So total 6 RTTs in case of persistent HTTP with pipeline.
For non-persistent with 5 parallel TCP:
- DNS lookup will take 3 RTTs.
- TCP connection: 2 RTTs (for opening webpage)
- HTTP request/response: Since we are using non-persistent HTTP, each TCP connection can be used to request one object at a time. Since there are 10 objects in total, and we have 5 parallel TCP connections, it will take 2 rounds of HTTP requests/responses to fetch all the objects. Therefore, this step will take a minimum of 4 RTTs (2 RTTs for the first round and 2 RTTs for the second round).
∴ 3 + 2 + 4 RTTs i.e. 9 RTTs
∴ option (c)
questions.explanation_html:
persistent http with pipelining will take 3 RTTs (one for connection + one for opening web page + one for 10 objects)
And in iterative mode for DNS is 3 RTTs (Root + Top domain + Authoritative).
So total 6 RTTs in case of persistent HTTP with pipeline.
For non-persistent with 5 parallel TCP:

  1. DNS lookup will take 3 RTTs.
  2. TCP connection: 2 RTTs (for opening webpage)
  3. HTTP request/response: Since we are using non-persistent HTTP, each TCP connection can be used to request one object at a time. Since there are 10 objects in total, and we have 5 parallel TCP connections, it will take 2 rounds of HTTP requests/responses to fetch all the objects. Therefore, this step will take a minimum of 4 RTTs (2 RTTs for the first round and 2 RTTs for the second round).
    ∴ 3 + 2 + 4 RTTs i.e. 9 RTTs
    ∴ option (c)

questions.question: Which of the following fields is/are modified in the IP header of a packet going out of a network address translation (NAT) device from an internal network to an external network? [ GATE|| 2024 || SET1||MSQ || 1-mark ]
questions.question_html: Which of the following fields is/are modified in the IP header of a packet going out of a network address translation (NAT) device from an internal network to an external network? [ GATE|| 2024 || SET1||MSQ || 1-mark ]
questions.explanation:
NAT device takes the outgoing packet and replaces the source IP address from private address pool to a IP address from its public address pool.
Since the source IP address is changed, the header checksum will also be recalculated so that the router does not get error while checking the header checksum.
Answer: B,D
questions.explanation_html:
NAT device takes the outgoing packet and replaces the source IP address from private address pool to a IP address from its public address pool.

Since the source IP address is changed, the header checksum will also be recalculated so that the router does not get error while checking the header checksum.

Answer: B,D

questions.question: Which of the following statements about IPv4 fragmentation is/are TRUE? [GATE||2024 ||SET2||MSQ || 1-mark]
questions.question_html: Which of the following statements about IPv4 fragmentation is/are TRUE? [GATE||2024 ||SET2||MSQ || 1-mark]

options.text: The fragmentation of an IP datagram is performed only at the source of the datagram.
options.html: The fragmentation of an IP datagram is performed only at the source of the datagram.

options.text: The reassembly of fragments is performed at all intermediate routers along the path from the source to the destination.
options.html: The reassembly of fragments is performed at all intermediate routers along the path from the source to the destination.

options.text: The reassembly of fragments is performed only at the destination of the datagram.
options.html: The reassembly of fragments is performed only at the destination of the datagram.

options.text: The fragmentation of an IP datagram is performed at any IP router which finds that the size of the datagram to be transmitted exceeds the MTU.
options.html: The fragmentation of an IP datagram is performed at any IP router which finds that the size of the datagram to be transmitted exceeds the MTU.

questions.explanation:
Option D:
Fragmentation is done by intermediary devices such as Routers .
When a packet needs to be fragmented because it exceeds the Maximum Transmission Unit (MTU) of a network segment, the original packet is divided into smaller fragments that fit within the MTU limits. The original packet's data is split into pieces, each typically including an IP header with appropriate fragmentation information.
Option C :
Reassembly of fragmented Datagrams is done only after reaching the destinatio n .  It cannot take place at any intermediary place because :
- All fragments may not meet at a router .
- Further fragmentation of fragmented packets can take place .
- fragmented datagrams may reach destination via an independent path .
questions.explanation_html:
Option D:

Fragmentation is done by intermediary devices such as Routers .

When a packet needs to be fragmented because it exceeds the Maximum Transmission Unit (MTU) of a network segment, the original packet is divided into smaller fragments that fit within the MTU limits. The original packet's data is split into pieces, each typically including an IP header with appropriate fragmentation information.

Option C :

Reassembly of fragmented Datagrams is done only after reaching the destination . It cannot take place at any intermediary place because : 

  1. All fragments may not meet at a router .
  2. Further fragmentation of fragmented packets can take place .
  3. fragmented datagrams may reach destination via an independent path .

questions.question:
An IP machine Q has a path to another IP machine H via three IP routers R1, R2, and R3.
Q — R1 — R2 — R3 — H
H acts as an HTTP server, and Q connects to H via HTTP and downloads a file. Session layer encryption is used, with DES as the shared key encryption protocol. Consider the following four pieces of information:
[ I1 ] The URL of the file downloaded by Q
[ I2 ] The TCP port numbers at Q and H
[ I3 ] The IP addresses of Q and H
[ I4 ] The link layer addresses of Q and H
Which of I1, I2, I3, and I4 can an intruder learn through sniffing at R2 alone? [GATE||2014|| Set 2||MCQ||2MARK ]
questions.question_html:
An IP machine Q has a path to another IP machine H via three IP routers R1, R2, and R3.
Q — R1 — R2 — R3 — H

H acts as an HTTP server, and Q connects to H via HTTP and downloads a file. Session layer encryption is used, with DES as the shared key encryption protocol. Consider the following four pieces of information:
[I1] The URL of the file downloaded by Q
[I2] The TCP port numbers at Q and H
[I3] The IP addresses of Q and H
[I4] The link layer addresses of Q and H

Which of I1, I2, I3, and I4 can an intruder learn through sniffing at R2 alone? [GATE||2014|| Set 2||MCQ||2MARK ]
questions.explanation:
I1 is encrypted by DES at the Session layer so intruder can't see that.
I2 is a part of TCP header(below Session layer), so it is not encrypted. Obv., the intruder can see that.
I3 is a part of the IP header(below TCP layer), so again, it is not encrypted and the intruder can see that as well.
I4 is not a part of the DLL header(below the IP layer), since the DLL always contains the Mac Addresses of the immediate sender(previous hop-R1) and the immediate destination(next hop-R3), so the intruder cannot see I4.
So, answer - (C)

questions.title: Computer Network || 2018 NAT || 2-mark || Module 4:Routing and Application Layer
questions.question:
Consider an IP packet with a length of 4500 bytes that includes a 20-byte IPv4 header and a 40-byte TCP header. The packet is forwarded to an IPv4 router that supports a Maximum Transmission Unit (MTU) of 600 bytes. Assume that the length of the IP header in all the outgoing fragments of this packet is 20 bytes. Assume that the fragmentation offset value stored in the first fragment is 0.
The fragmentation offset value stored in the third fragment is __________. [GATE|||| 2018 NAT || 2-mark]
questions.question_html:
Consider an IP packet with a length of 4500 bytes that includes a 20-byte IPv4 header and a 40-byte TCP header. The packet is forwarded to an IPv4 router that supports a Maximum Transmission Unit (MTU) of 600 bytes. Assume that the length of the IP header in all the outgoing fragments of this packet is 20 bytes. Assume that the fragmentation offset value stored in the first fragment is 0.

The fragmentation offset value stored in the third fragment is __________. [GATE|||| 2018 NAT || 2-mark]
questions.explanation:
IP length = 4500B. Payload = 4480B.
MTU = 600B.
So, max packet we can make here is: Header(20) + Payload (580). But the payload isn't a multiple of 8 here.
Hence legally the max packet here is: Header(20) + Payload(576).
Seven such packets make up a total payload of 4032B.
So, last packet would be Header(20) + Payload (448).
Offset of first packet = 0.
Offset of second packet = 576B = 72
Offset of third packet = 72 + 72 = 144.
questions.explanation_html:
IP length = 4500B. Payload = 4480B.

MTU = 600B.

So, max packet we can make here is: Header(20) + Payload (580). But the payload isn't a multiple of 8 here.

Hence legally the max packet here is: Header(20) + Payload(576).

Seven such packets make up a total payload of 4032B.

 

So, last packet would be Header(20) + Payload (448).

 

Offset of first packet = 0.

Offset of second packet = 576B = 72

Offset of third packet = 72 + 72 = 144.

The minimum number of TCP connections required to display the web page completely in your browser is _________. [GATE || 2020 NAT || 1-mark ]
questions.question_html: Assume that you have made a request for a web page through your web browser to a web server. Initially, the browser cache is empty. Further, the browser is configured to send HTTP requests in non-persistent mode. The web page contains text and five very small images. The minimum number of TCP connections required to display the web page completely in your browser is _________. [GATE || 2020 NAT || 1-mark ]
questions.explanation:
In non-persistent HTTP, for every object, there is a TCP connection required.
Hence, 1 TCP connection for text and 5 TCP connections for images are required.
So, a total of 6 connections are required.
questions.explanation_html:
In non-persistent HTTP, for every object, there is a TCP connection required.
Hence, 1 TCP connection for text and 5 TCP connections for images are required.
So, a total of 6 connections are required.

questions.title: Computer Network || 2021 MSQ || 2-mark || Module 4:Routing and Application Layer
questions.question:
Consider a computer network using the distance vector routing algorithm in its network layer. The partial topology of the network is as shown below.
The objective is to find the shortest-cost path from the router R to routers P and Q . Assume that R does not initially know the shortest routes to P and Q . Assume that R has three neighbouring routers denoted as X , Y , and Z . During one iteration, R measures its distance to its neighbours X , Y , and Z as 3, 2, and 5, respectively. Router R gets routing vectors from its neighbours that indicate that the distance to router P from routers X , Y , and Z are 7, 6, and 5, respectively. The routing vector also indicates that the distance to router Q from routers X , Y , and Z are 4, 6, and 8, respectively. Which of the following statement(s) is/are correct with respect to the new routing table of R , after update during this iteration? [GATE|| 2021 || SET2||MSQ || 2-mark]
questions.question_html:
Consider a computer network using the distance vector routing algorithm in its network layer. The partial topology of the network is as shown below.

The objective is to find the shortest-cost path from the router R to routers P and Q. Assume that R does not initially know the shortest routes to P and Q. Assume that R has three neighbouring routers denoted as X, Y, and Z. During one iteration, R measures its distance to its neighbours X, Y, and Z as 3, 2, and 5, respectively. Router R gets routing vectors from its neighbours that indicate that the distance to router P from routers X, Y, and Z are 7, 6, and 5, respectively. The routing vector also indicates that the distance to router Q from routers X, Y, and Z are 4, 6, and 8, respectively. Which of the following statement(s) is/are correct with respect to the new routing table of R, after update during this iteration? [GATE|| 2021 || SET2||MSQ || 2-mark]

options.text: The distance from R to Q will be stored as 7.
options.html: The distance from R to Q will be stored as 7.

options.text: The distance from R to P will be stored as 10.
options.html: The distance from R to P will be stored as 10.

options.text: The next hop router for a packet from R to Q is Z .
options.html: The next hop router for a packet from R to Q is Z.

options.text: The next hop router for a packet from R to P is Y .
options.html: The next hop router for a packet from R to P is Y.

questions.explanation:
Given R gets the distance vector (3, 2, 5). After the one iteration, the distance vector from X to P , Y to P , and Z to P is (7, 6, 5) respectively. The distance vector from R to P via XYZ is (3 + 7, 2 + 6, 5 + 5) = (10, 8, 10).
So, take the minimum distance from R to P which is 8 via Y .
After the iteration, the distance vector from X to Q , Z to Q is (4, 6, 8) respectively. The distance vector from R to Q via XYZ is (3 + 4, 2 + 6, 5 + 8) = (7, 8, 13).
So, take the minimum distance from R to Q which is 7 via X .
questions.explanation_html:
Given R gets the distance vector (3, 2, 5). After the one iteration, the distance vector from X to P, Y to P, and Z to P is (7, 6, 5) respectively. The distance vector from R to P via XYZ is (3 + 7, 2 + 6, 5 + 5) = (10, 8, 10).
So, take the minimum distance from R to P which is 8 via Y.
After the iteration, the distance vector from X to Q, Z to Q is (4, 6, 8) respectively. The distance vector from R to Q via XYZ is (3 + 4, 2 + 6, 5 + 8) = (7, 8, 13).
So, take the minimum distance from R to Q which is 7 via X.

questions.title: Computer Network || 2022 NAT || 2-mark || Module 4:Routing and Application Layer
questions.question:
Consider a network with three routers P, Q, R shown in the figure below. All the links have cost of unity.
The routers exchange distance vector routing information and have converged on the routing tables, after which the link Q-R fails. Assume that P and Q send out routing updates at random times, each at the same average rate. The probability of a routing loop formation (rounded off to one decimal place) between P and Q, leading to the count-to-infinity problem, is ___________. [GATE||2022 NAT || 2-mark]
questions.question_html:
Consider a network with three routers P, Q, R shown in the figure below. All the links have cost of unity.

The routers exchange distance vector routing information and have converged on the routing tables, after which the link Q-R fails. Assume that P and Q send out routing updates at random times, each at the same average rate. The probability of a routing loop formation (rounded off to one decimal place) between P and Q, leading to the count-to-infinity problem, is ___________. [GATE||2022 NAT || 2-mark]
questions.explanation:
It is given that P and Q are sending routing updates at the same average rate, so in a particular time period of sending updates, there are two possibilities, one is P sends before Q and other is Q sends before P.
Now, We have 2 conditions:
- Q sends update to P first, then P finds the value of R as ∞ and count-to-infinity will not occur.
- P sends update to Q first, then Q found that P has a way to reach R, so it is count-to-infinity problem.
As Q - R is broken, So R cannot send update to anyone.
From above two conditions (total possibilities), only one condition leads to count-to-infinity problem and each possibility has equal probability of occurrence, so probability is 1/2 = 0.5 for count-to-infinity.
questions.explanation_html:
It is given that P and Q are sending routing updates at the same average rate, so in a particular time period of sending updates, there are two possibilities, one is P sends before Q and other is Q sends before P.
Now, We have 2 conditions:

  1. Q sends update to P first, then P finds the value of R as ∞ and count-to-infinity will not occur.
  2. P sends update to Q first, then Q found that P has a way to reach R, so it is count-to-infinity problem.
    As Q - R is broken, So R cannot send update to anyone.
    From above two conditions (total possibilities), only one condition leads to count-to-infinity problem and each possibility has equal probability of occurrence, so probability is 1/2 = 0.5 for count-to-infinity.

questions.question:
Identify the ONE CORRECT matching between the OSI layers and their corresponding functionalities as shown. [GATE ||2025||SET1|| MCQ || 1-mark]
OSI Layers Functionalities (a) Network layer (I) Packet routing (b) Transport layer (II) Framing and error handling (c) Datalink layer (III) Host to host communication
questions.question_html:
Identify the ONE CORRECT matching between the OSI layers and their corresponding functionalities as shown. [GATE ||2025||SET1|| MCQ || 1-mark]

OSI LayersFunctionalities
(a) Network layer(I) Packet routing
(b) Transport layer(II) Framing and error handling
(c) Datalink layer(III) Host to host communication
questions.explanation:
- Packet Routing is the responsibility of the Network Layer (a) (I).
- Framing and error (bit level) handling is the responsibility of the Data Link Layer. Hence, (c) (II).
- Host-to-Host communication (end-to-end Communication) is the responsibility of the Transport Layer (Practically). Hence, (b) (III).
Therefore, option (b) is correct.
questions.explanation_html:
• Packet Routing is the responsibility of the Network Layer (a) (I).
  • Framing and error (bit level) handling is the responsibility of the Data Link Layer. Hence, (c) (II).
  • Host-to-Host communication (end-to-end Communication) is the responsibility of the Transport Layer (Practically). Hence, (b) (III).

Therefore, option (b) is correct.

questions.title: Computer Network || 2025 MCQ || 1-mark || Module 4:Routing and Application Layer
questions.question:
A machine receives an IPv4 datagram. The protocol field of the IPv4 header has the protocol number of a protocol X.
Which ONE of the following is NOT a possible candidate for X? [GATE|| 2025 MCQ || 1-mark]
questions.question_html:
A machine receives an IPv4 datagram. The protocol field of the IPv4 header has the protocol number of a protocol X.
Which ONE of the following is NOT a possible candidate for X? [GATE|| 2025 MCQ || 1-mark]
questions.explanation:
- RIP (Routing Information Protocol) - Uses UDP (Port 520)
- RIP is a routing protocol but does NOT have a direct protocol number in the IPv4 header. Instead, RIP uses UDP as its transport layer protocol (UDP port 520). Since RIP is encapsulated inside UDP, it does NOT appear directly in the Protocol field of an IPv4 header.
IPv4 Protocol field value = 1 - ICMP
IPv4 Protocol field value = 2 - IGMP
IPv4 Protocol field value = 89 - OSPF
